request.question: What type of criminals, named after a term from the Revolutionary War, were involved in the Clay County Savings Association Building robbery?

title: Clay County Savings Association Building
Edina Double Square Historic District
Bushwhacker
Clay County Courthouse (Alabama)
Claycomo, Missouri
Savings Bank Building
Old Clay County Courthouse (Clay, West Virginia)
Onondaga County Savings Bank
Clay County, Tennessee
Clay County Courthouse (Illinois)
sentences: The Clay County Savings Association in Liberty, Missouri, was robbed on February 13, 1866, making it one of the earliest documented daylight bank robberies.
The robbers escaped with at least $60,000 ( ) and killed a bystander outside.
The robbery is believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas, possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement, which became known as the James–Younger Gang.
Edina Double Square Historic District is a national historic district located at Edina, Knox County, Missouri.
The district encompasses 37 contributing buildings in the central business district of Edina.
It developed between about 1865 and 1945 and includes representative examples of Italianate and Streamline Moderne style architecture.

Notable contributing buildings include the Public Works Administration funded Knox County Courthouse (1934-1935) designed by William B. Ittner, Bishoff Bakery (1891), Northern Hotel (1860s), Ennis House/Northern Hotel (c. 1865), Edina School and Gymnasium (1915-1916), D. H. Mudd Building (c. 1904), Phillip Linville Building (c. 1908, c. 1963), Tobias J. Lycan Building (c. 1891), Jacob Pugh Building (c. 1891), Albert G. Bostick Building (c. 1891), Knox County Savings Bank (c. 1891), Thomas Burk Buildings (c. 1881), Bank of Edina Building (c. 1907), Joseph F. Biggerstaff Buildings (c. 1881), Stablein Building (c. 1870), and Knox County Public Library (c. 1915).

Bushwhacking was a form of guerrilla warfare common during the American Revolutionary War, American Civil War and other conflicts in which there were large areas of contested land and few governmental resources to control these tracts.
This was particularly prevalent in rural areas during the Civil War where there were sharp divisions between those favoring the Union and Confederacy in the conflict.
The perpetrators of the attacks were called bushwhackers.
The term "bushwhacking" is still in use today to describe ambushes done with the aim of attrition.
The Clay County Courthouse is a historic courthouse building in Ashland, Clay County, Alabama.
The Classical Revival-style building has served as the county courthouse since its completion in 1906.
Notable characteristics include its large dome, with clocks inset on four sides, and cupola surmounted by a statuary representing justice.
It was added to the National Register of Historic Places on November 21, 1976.
Claycomo is a village in Clay County, Missouri, United States.
The village’s name is a portmanteau of "Clay, Co" (Clay County), and "Mo" (Missouri).
The population was 1,430 at the 2010 census.
Ford Motor Company has an assembly plant in Claycomo.
Kansas City Assembly is responsible for building the Ford F-150 pickup trucks and the Ford Transit vans.
The Savings Bank Building is a commercial building located at 101 South Front Street in Marquette, Michigan.
It is also known as the Marquette County Savings Bank.
The building was designated a Michigan State Historic Site in 1976 and listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1978.
The Old Clay County Courthouse in Clay, West Virginia was designed by Frank L. Packard and built in 1902.
The Beaux-Arts building was located on a hill overlooking the county seat.

The courthouse was the site of three notable trials: the Sarah Ann Legg trial of 1905, the first trial of a woman in Clay County for murder, the Booger Hole trial of 1917, in which citizens nearly lynched the defendants, and the Oscar Bail trial of 1953, in which Bail was convicted of killing a mine guard in the Great Widen Coal Strike.
The Onondaga County Savings Bank was chartered in 1855 in Syracuse, New York.
It was a franchise ahead of its time and had four separate branches by the late 19th century.
The "Onondaga County Savings Bank Building" was constructed in 1896 adjacent to the Erie Canal in "Clinton Square".
Clay County is a county located in the U.S. state of Tennessee.
As of the 2010 census, the population was 7,861.
Its county seat and only incorporated city is Celina.
Clay County is named in honor of American statesman Henry Clay, member of the United States Senate from Kentucky and United States Secretary of State in the 19th century.
Its current mayor is Dale Reagan.
The Clay County Courthouse, located at 300 Broadway Street in Louisville, is the county courthouse serving Clay County, Illinois.
Built in 1913, the courthouse was Clay County's fourth courthouse; it has served continuously as the seat of Clay County government since its opening.
Architect Joseph W. Royer, who planned several other Illinois courthouses, designed the Classical Revival building.
The courthouse was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 2015.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: What type of criminals, named after a term from the Revolutionary War, were involved in the Clay County Savings Association Building robbery?
hop1_docs: Clay County Savings Association Building | The Clay County Savings Association in Liberty, Missouri, was robbed on February 13, 1866, making it one of the earliest documented daylight bank robberies.  The robbers escaped with at least $60,000 ( ) and killed a bystander outside.  The robbery is believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas, possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement, which became known as the James–Younger Gang.

Aryan Republican Army | The Aryan Republican Army is the name given to white supremacist criminal group active in the United States in the early to mid-1990s.  The group is alleged to have associated with convicted bomber Timothy McVeigh in the months before the Oklahoma City bombing.  The organization was sometimes referenced in the media as the Midwest Bank Robbers.  The group was created by Peter Langan and Richard Lee Guthrie in 1992.  The group considered themselves a leaderless organization, meaning that there was no true leader to the group and that they were more of a group of associated people acting together to achieve their goals.  Although the group was mainly a criminal enterprise, they did have a terrorist like agenda.  With the money they were able to steal in the 22 confirmed robberies they were involved in, the group began stockpiling weapons and ammunition.  It is believed that the group wished to start a race war with the weapons that were stockpiled.  The group began to fall apart as members of the Aryan Republican Army were arrested after one of the former members of the group became an informant as part of a plea bargain.  By mid-1996, practically all known members had been apprehended and the group was in shambles.

State Line Mob | The State Line Mob was an association of criminal elements that operated in the 1950s and 1960s at the Mississippi–Tennessee state line in Alcorn County, Mississippi, and McNairy County, Tennessee, along U.S. Route 45.  The State Line Mob was involved in bootlegging, gambling, prostitution, tourist fleecing, robbery, and murder.  A few of the members were from Phenix City, Alabama, having been displaced from that town when martial law was declared by the Governor and the Alabama National Guard attempted to clean the town up.

Florencia 13 | The Florencia 13 is an American criminal street gang in Los Angeles, California, composed mainly of Hispanic-Americans and some blacks.  The gangs name is after the Florence area of Los Angeles county, the 13 is for it's allegiance to the Mexican Mafia.  They are involved in drug smuggling, murder, assault, robbery etc.

Arifs (gang) | The Arifs are a South East London-based Turkish Cypriot criminal organization heavily involved in armed robbery, contract killing, drug trafficking and other racketeering-related activities within London's underworld since the late 1960s.  Following the downfall of the Kray brothers, the Arifs were one of several criminal organizations who took control of the London underworld including the Clerkenwell crime syndicate and the Brindle family with whom they were engaged in a highly publicized gangland war during the 1990s.

Quaker Springs, New York | Quaker Springs is a hamlet in Saratoga County.  The zip code is 12871.  Quaker Springs is between NY-32 Route 4.  Quaker Springs United Methodist Church is located in the hamlet.  An anti-slavery society was based in Quaker Springs and several possible participants in the Underground Railroad were in Quaker Springs.  The name Quaker Springs came from a type of mineral spring located in the area.  The area including Quaker Springs was involved in Revolutionary War positions.
U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II | U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II
hop1_titles: U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II
response.summary_1: Evidence: passage notes the robbery "was believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas" linked to Jesse James, Archie Clement, and the James–Younger Gang. Bridge entity: James–Younger Gang (and individuals Jesse James; Archie Clement). Missing fact: the specific Revolutionary-War-derived term used to name these criminals (the "type" asked for) is not present in the passages. Retrieval directions: (1) open the "James–Younger Gang" page (or its Wikipedia article) to check how the robbers are labeled (search the article text for terms like "bushwhacker," "banditti," or "guerrilla") and any note on term origin; (2) if unclear, open the biographies of "Jesse James" and "Archie Clement" to confirm the label and its etymology (search queries: "James–Younger Gang" bushwhacker OR banditti site:wikipedia.org; "Jesse James" bushwhacker site:wikipedia.org). Clarification: none needed unless you meant a different person than those named.
response.hop2_query:
site:wikipedia.org "James–Younger Gang" bushwhacker OR bushwhackers OR banditti OR guerrilla
site:wikipedia.org "Jesse James" bushwhacker OR bushwhackers OR banditti OR guerrilla
site:wikipedia.org "Archie Clement" bushwhacker OR bushwhackers OR banditti OR guerrilla
hop2_docs: Bushwhacker | Bushwhacking was a form of guerrilla warfare common during the American Revolutionary War, American Civil War and other conflicts in which there were large areas of contested land and few governmental resources to control these tracts.  This was particularly prevalent in rural areas during the Civil War where there were sharp divisions between those favoring the Union and Confederacy in the conflict.  The perpetrators of the attacks were called bushwhackers.  The term "bushwhacking" is still in use today to describe ambushes done with the aim of attrition.

Jesse James | Jesse Woodson James (September 5, 1847April 3, 1882) was an American outlaw, bank and train robber, guerrilla, and leader of the James–Younger Gang.  Raised in the "Little Dixie" area of western Missouri, James and his family maintained strong Southern sympathies.  He and his brother Frank James joined pro-Confederate guerrillas known as "bushwhackers" operating in Missouri and Kansas during the American Civil War.  As followers of William Quantrill and "Bloody Bill" Anderson, they were accused of participating in atrocities against Union soldiers and civilian abolitionists, including the Centralia Massacre in 1864.
Bushwhacked! | Bushwhacked!  is an Australian children's adventure television series aired on ABC3 on 6 October 2012.
Bushwhackers (disambiguation) | The words bushwhacker and bushwacker (spellings are often used interchangeably) may refer to:
Arizona Bushwhackers | Arizona Bushwhackers is a 1968 Western film starring Howard Keel and Yvonne de Carlo.
Bushwhacked (Firefly) | "Bushwhacked" is the third episode of the science fiction television series "Firefly" created by Joss Whedon.
The Bushwhackers (film) | The Bushwhackers is a 1925 Australian silent film directed by Raymond Longford loosely based on Alfred Tennyson's "Enoch Arden".  It is considered a lost film.
response.summary_2: The robbers were described as "bushwhackers." Contemporary sources identify Jesse James, Archie Clement, and members of the James–Younger Gang as pro-Confederate guerrillas commonly called bushwhackers—a term for guerrilla fighters that dates back to the Revolutionary War.
